sentences.text: Yeah, I mean, I was there for, for, on a trial basis.
And that was, like I said, something I always wanted to do.
And I was totally excited about it and, and loved that school.
But that was the only school that I really loved.
I mean, the others, you know, there's some things I like, some things I didn't like.
There was nothing about the school I didn't like, even the fact that I had to take maybe two buses and, and a subway in order to get there.
[LAUGHS] That didn't bother me.
I mean, as long as, as long as I was allowed.
Anyway, they took me there on, as I said, trial basis, and at the end of three months-- I, I don't know if it was three months or four.
After the first term I was called in by the principal.
In England, they call them the headmaster.
And he said he was, you know, that I-- it was OK for me to stay, that I have progressed.
In fact, I have a letter from him I, I just discovered.
I know I had it.
I have-- written by him as a, as a recommendation, a real good letter.
What happened was I had this scholarship, and I was torn between making a decision when I was, when I was notified by the Youth Aliyah that I can go to-- immigrate to Israel.
And when I first was told about it I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't respond positive because I just started my art training, and I, I can always go to Israel later, I thought.
But then they came back with me with this story, that my brother was 17, and if we wait another year, he'll be 18.
And that doesn't qualify for Youth Aliyah.
You have to be under 18.
You have to be under 18.
And they refused to send him by himself because they didn't want to separate us because we were always together, or they didn't think it was a good idea.
So I was kind of pressured into making a decision.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, I guess partially for my brother's sake, you know, I, I made that decision.
And they also said that they would-- you know, they have art schools in, in Israel and Jerusalem, Bezalel, which is a great art school.
And there's a good chance that they would send me to art school.
Well, that didn't happen, [LAUGHS] you know.
Ended up, we went to Israel, which is something, of course, I was always-- I always wanted to go.
I had a passion to go to Israel, just like I had a passion to, to do art.
And it was a difficult decision for me, but I made it, hoping I'll pick up on the art somehow later on.
And I tried.
I tried.
I, I took art courses in Israel.
I took art courses wherever I could, but I missed a great opportunity.
I had the scholarship at a great school in London.
And I remember the principal told me when-- you'll be sorry, he said to me.
Yeah.
Because it was-- but I didn't have nobody else to advise me.
I had no parents, nobody else.
[SIGHS] You know, it's funny.
We never considered ourselves as orphans because there was always that hope that our parents were alive somewhere.
But basically, that's the way we grew up, like orphans, you know.
I mean, you know, there was no contact with parents after that.
And I was watching [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the TV the other day about some kid said [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so many years the kid wasn't hugged or something like that.
Never dawned on me.
I grew up without that.
[LAUGHS] And yet I don't think-- there's so many people that use that as an excuse for their behavior today.
And heck, I don't know any of the kids that I went with ended up being nuts or maniacs or, or violent.
In fact, even the-- look, I was fortunate not to be in a concentration camp.
Even the, the concentration survivors, there's no one that could have lived through worse than that, and most of them, they're, they're OK.

I mean, they're emotionally, you know, scarred and everything else, but they don't take it out on, on, on, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] behavior that's not rational or anything like that or not accepted.
So I don't accept that, saying my parents' fault, something like that.
[LAUGHS] That doesn't make sense to me.
So where are we now?
Now we are-- Did you, did you and your brother kind of look to each other as sort of the guidance or, you know, to--
Guidance?
Yeah.
I guess we used to talk things over, yeah.
I mean, I always acknowledged him as being older and, and smarter than me, even though it was only one year.
He used to tease me a lot of times, too.
He has a little bit of my father's, [LAUGHS] you know, character.
In fact, I remember when we got to be adolescents and he claimed he knew everything about sex.
And I'd ask him to tell me.
And he'd say, no, when you get older, he said to me.
[LAUGHS] You know, he'd tease the hell out of me, you know.
Yeah.
We didn't know where to go with this.
The way I discovered the family, actually, is when we came to Israel.
There was three of us.
We were sent to a, actually, to a youth kibbutz school like, you know, for training, youth training.
And--
A friend of ours, Natan.
He came on the same boat to Israel.
He's from France, actually, but we, we kind of met on the boat, whatever, and we became very close friends.
And we kind of stuck together, the three of us.
And when we were sent to this youth training kibbutz, we rebelled.
[LAUGHS] I mean, we came to Israel as pioneers, you know.
We wanted to start building and everything else, so we refused, and we ran away, OK?
And we went to the authorities, the-- you know, like, the, that handle new immigrants.
And we said, no way.
We don't want to go back to school.
We finished school.
So reluctantly, they sent us to a kibbutz in the Negev, which is like the worst place you can send kids.
I think the reason they sent us over there instead of sending us into a cushy kibbutz that was established that we'll probably say, yuck, you know, and say that's not for me.
I'll go back to school.
Well, they were wrong, because we loved it.
Now when was this? 19--
This was is '59.
Yeah.
I mean '49, yeah.
A year after War of Independence.
So we went to this kibbutz, and we felt accepted.
And we felt, you know-- we felt we were doing something, you know, significant and-- no, we loved it.
They were wrong about thinking that we'd chicken out.
What were you doing?
In fact, I was 16 years old.
And one of the first things-- it was right after the War of Independence.
One of the first things they did is they teach you how to protect yourself, because it was still at a time where there were shootings going on.
We lived real-- we lived, like, two miles from the border, from the Egyptian border, you know, of the Gaza Strip.
You could see Gaza on the horizon, you know.
It was-- that's how close it was, OK?
And there would be raids at nighttime in the camp.
We had mines around, around the kibbutz still from the War of Independence for protection.
So, anyway, one of the first things they did is taught us how, how to use weapons.
And I remember the first five bullets that I shot into, into a target were five bullseyes.
And I was a celebrity.
[LAUGHS]
They called me in Hebrew [HEBREW], which means sharpshooter.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So yeah, I remember that.
And my friend Natan, who was also, you know, on the shooting practice, he got four bullseyes.
He was good, too, but I beat him.
And then my brother, I forget what he had.
He wasn't as good.
He's, yeah, right between me and my brother, like six months older than me, yeah.
Well, my brother grew up, he had-- whoops.
Here we go again.
He had this training.
And he enjoyed agriculture.
I didn't.
So when they put me on the kibbutz to work in agriculture, I complained.
I said I wanted to work in construction, something that I can see I'm building.
It's funny.
And they said to me, you're too young.
That's too hard work for a 16- year-old, you know, to dig trenches, this and that, you know, drag cement around.
Because, you know, everything was, was very manual in those days.
There wasn't too much automation, you know.
So it was hard work.
And they kept refusing to, to allow me to work, said I was too young.
They finally gave in, and I worked in construction.
And it was hard work, but I liked it.
Yeah, I did.
Anywhere from digging trenches to cement mixing to putting on tiles on the roof, carpentry-- anything that I saw that was significant progress, to me, that was very rewarding.
Oh, it was like the wild west, then, in those days.
If we were sent to the field-- I'm 16 years old.
I'm running around with an automatic weapon.
That's the way I used to go to the fields to work.
I thought it was, it was hilarious, you know.
I thought it was a very exciting life.
[LAUGHS] I mean, yeah, not saying I wasn't scared once in a while, but it was a very exciting environment, you know.
But--
Yeah.
I mean, I was real gung-ho about that.
And, yeah, we were in the kibbutz about two years.
And my friend Natan, he's one of the first went into the service.
He was drafted.
At the time they didn't draft all the, all the eligible kids that got-- became 18.
They did draft all the kids in Israel, but if you were on a border kibbutz, they didn't draft-- they only drafted a, a percentage of them, because they thought they needed them there.
So Natan was drafted.
And then I think shortly after that, I just volunteered, [LAUGHS] you know, because they would have had to draft somebody.
And I don't know how they would pick somebody-- either me or my brother or whoever.
I just volunteered.
And we ended up-- I remember when I first found out he volunteered for the, for the airborne, I laughed so hard.
I said, you dummy.
And I-- you know, that's going to be the worst thing you could have done.
Make a long story short, that's where I ended up, too, in the airborne.
[LAUGHS] That was peer pressure, I think.
Because I, I was, I was with a group of kids that were pretty gung-ho.
And they volunteered.
And I just went with them.
That's OK.
Oh.
Oh, how I discovered.
OK.
Going back a while.
While we're in the kibbutz, there was-- I can't think of his name.
It's not important, I guess.
He was also from Czechoslovakia, one of, one of the fellows in the kibbutz.
And he must've recognized that name, Pinkasovic.
I mean, it's not a very common name.
And he did some checking.
And he located a first cousin of ours in the Petah Tikva area, which is a little bit north of Tel Aviv.
She was a first cousin.
And her mother, I think, and my mother were sisters, OK?
And of course, you know, we, we got together or something, arranged to meet, went to visit her.
And you know, we were suspicious of anything like that being true and, you know, weren't accepting it right away.
But gradually, by asking questions and stuff, we, we realized, I guess it's true.
Her name was Gittel, and her married name was Teumann.
You know, I don't remember her, her-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember her maiden name, although her-- she has a brother in Cleveland, and his last name was Pearl.
But I don't know that was her maiden name.
I, I really don't know.
He might've changed it.
Yeah.
Well, her mother and my mother were sisters, OK?
So anyway, she was older than us.
She was married, and she had two, two daughters at the time, small girls.
And through her-- she was old enough to have maintained contact.
She was a survivor from, from, from the war in Europe.
And she was old enough to know that they had relatives in United States that were born here.
So she's the one that contacted them.
And, and through her, that's how I got to know I got all these umpteen relatives in Cleveland and New York.
And-- But how did you find out about your, your parents and, and your family back there?
Well, yeah, I'd ask them what they know about our parents.
And all they could tell me is that they, they were in this ghetto, that that's where we got the letter from.
And towards the end of the war, or the latter part of the war, they were taken to concentration camps.
And that was the last they heard.
And they, they just assumed that if they don't come out, they're not alive.
No, I don't remember them saying what camp.
No.
Well, logically, if they're alive, they knew where I was, right?
I mean, they sent us to England.
We weren't hard to find.
For me to try to locate them, it's kind of impossible.
Also you, you have to understand the environment that, that we came from, in the beginning-- I don't even have a birth certificate.
I mean, there's-- we didn't harp too much on this name thing.
One of the theories that I just seem to think is the reason that, that-- did I say anything that-- you know, how I got from Lux-- from Pinkasovic to Lux?
OK, I found this out from, from an aunt that is my, my father's sister.
Her name is Gizella Lux.
And she's the one who told me your name is not Pinkasovic.
It's Lux.
That was your father's name.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it just seems to me, possibly, the reason that they used my mother's maiden name is because we never registered with the authorities on, on certificates.
I mean, we lived in the sticks.
You know, we-- you go to the city and register.
I mean, where?
What city, you know?
And possibly, my father was avoiding the draft, too.
I don't know.
Because he, he never served the Czech military.
I always wondered that time that we were stopped at the railroad, if that had something to do with it.
But maybe she just lost tickets.
But it's possible that he, he was avoiding the draft, and-- I don't know.
I'm not sure.
Yeah, it has my, my mother's name on it, Pinkasovic, yeah.
That's the, the name we went by.
In fact, I mentioned this earlier before that while I was growing up in England, I always wondered if that was our true name because I never heard anybody with that kind of name.
[LAUGHS] So I thought some, some bureaucrat just put that down and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, you know, I just went by my first name.
As a, as a five, six-year-old, you might not really know what your real name is, you know?
Isador?
Yeah, Isador Pinkasovic.
Yeah.
Oh, it was, it was quite an, quite an experience, and-- so anyway, there was basically just two, two other relatives there that maintained communication in the mail with us.
One was my aunt, Gizella, and the other one was Goodmans, which was on my mother's side.
Also in Cleveland.
And the ones that were born here, they were kind of distant.
They didn't feel it was that significant to find another cousin, [LAUGHS] you know?
It's the ones that survived are the ones that try to keep in touch.

And it was through her efforts, my Aunt Gizella, my father's sister, that she's the one that tried to encourage me to come to the United States, and-- which I really wasn't planning to do, except while I was in the service, I filled out a form for immigration.
And I kind of forgot about it.
And it wasn't until years later that my quota came up because I, I had to come on the Czech quota because I was born in Czechoslovakia.
So it was years before that came up and had I-- if I was married at the time, maybe I wouldn't have come.
But I was single.
And I said, what the heck.
I'll try.
So you had to go by your country of birth, not the Israeli--
Yes.
Absolutely, yeah.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
It's automatic.
[LAUGHS] There's a-- Israeli citizenship is automatic.

One thing that was kind of significant when I came to the United States-- and I always said I'm glad my brother wasn't the one that came here because he would've been totally [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] emotionally upset about what happened is, when I came here, I got a cold shoulder from relatives, yeah, except a couple, you know, the ones that survived.

But all the American-- it's, it's like I, I, I came after-- I got-- I tried to, to, to excuse them for their, their attitude, like how many relatives already came from Europe that they showed, you know, enthusiasm or were excited about.
After a while, it gets to be old, old, you know, old hat, you know.
And by the time I came, it's not a big deal.
And for me it was-- all my life I didn't have a living relative except my brother.
And to discover there's was such a, such a, you know, a-- something to be really get excited about and emotional about.
And when I got this cold shoulder when I came here, it was really a slap in the face.
In fact, I remember I have one relative in Cleveland, very, very wealthy.
And it seems like the more money they have, they think that's what everybody's looking at.
I never asked anybody for anything.
[LAUGHS] Even my aunt in New York-- I ended up in Cleveland.
I think I'll come to that later.
I didn't leave New York till I paid her back for the, for the transportation that, that she put-- laid out.
Yeah, Aunt Gizella.
And that's the way I am.

No, I didn't come to America to take advantage of rich relatives, but they seem to have had-- they were very-- they put on this defensive attitude, you know, of all these new immigrants, I think, maybe because of the track record that some of them, you know, you know, did, did ask for-- you know, you're American.
Help me, you know.
In fact, I have-- back to this very wealthy cousin that I have.
After I was introduced to him, which had to have a special appointment, you know, made a big deal out of it.
After that, when I, when I met him the second time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she said to me, yeah, I, I remember you.
You're what's, what's-her-name's cousin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The cousin that introduced me to, to her and her husband, I'm related to her exactly the same way as, as to them.
And she's, yeah, I remember you.
You're what's-her-name's cousin.
She didn't acknowledge that I was her cousin, you know.
And, and--
Well, I was hurt many times in Cleveland by American-born relatives who really didn't care.
But you know, I've tried to put myself in, in their position, try to understand them.
And I don't know how I would've been if I would've been born here, don't quite grasp the how others feel, so I-- but I don't-- I'm glad my brother didn't have to go through it.
That's all.
'58, I believe.
Late '58, yeah.
Uh huh.
Yeah.
I came to New York.
My, my Aunt Gizella, she, she had a luncheonette in Brooklyn, Williamsburg, probably the filthiest place I've seen on Earth.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
The, the minute I, I got off the plane, they were already asking me, how do you like America?
And I'm knee deep in, in dirt, you know, and trash.
So that was my first impression.
But I would always say, well, you know, I will, I will be polite.
I wouldn't-- I'd say, well, I haven't seen anything yet, so it's hard to say, you know.
And, yeah, she picked me up at the airport and took me straight to the luncheonette, put on a apron, and I was in America.
[LAUGHS]
Eight months, yeah.
Oops.
Cleveland, yeah.
And in New York [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess my aunt thought I was going to be like an adopted child because they didn't have any children.
Her husband had lost his family in the war.
She had lost a child.
And this was a, a late marriage.
They got married, I think, in Cleveland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shortly before that.
And then they, they moved to New York and opened up a business, you know, a luncheonette, and they were doing quite well.
They worked hard, though.
I have to give them credit.
And I just wasn't comfortable being an adopted child at the age of 25.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started going out and then meeting people.
And, and they didn't like that.
And there was friction building up and stuff.
So I-- in fact, while I was there, they kept telling me that America was a rough country.
You have to work, work hard.
And when they came, they worked for dollar an hour and all that in factories and God knows what, you know, sewing like most immigrants did from Europe.
I came to United States.
I knew the language.
I had a good profession, and I found a job very easily.
I made a few calls.
I figured, I don't know my way around.
So I called an agency, an employment agency, found me a job almost instantly.
My first job was NYU at the university in the printing department as a cameraman.
It was the easiest job I ever had in my life.
And that's how I came to America to work hard.
They don't pay much, but it was a great job.
Good environment, friendly and everything.
Oh, fine.
Fine.
Oh, no, no.
No.
English was no-- I had no problem with the language.
No, they were-- at work treated me great.
It was a great environment.
No problem at all.
And I only stayed in New York, which I didn't like.
Even though I, I lived in London with all that congestion and everything, it was a totally different environment than New York.
New York to me is like a jungle.
[LAUGHS] I mean, I always compared the subways, because, you know, I used to travel the subways in London a lot.
And you stand in the subways like sardines.
You can't even move.
Yet the English, they just read their paper calmly, quiet.
In New York, you can get killed.
Everybody's pushing and shoving trying to get into the door when the, when the train opens up.
You can get, you can get thrown in front of a train.
And it's totally different there.
No.
[LAUGHS]
And so I just stayed in New York long enough to pay my-- like I said, to give my aunt back the money she, she put out, which I appreciated.
I always thanked her for it.
And--
Well, I visited Cleveland a couple of times, and I liked it better than New York.
So I decided even before that once I settled with my aunt, I'm going to move to Cleveland, which I did.
